title: Paint Coverage Calculator: Liters per Room 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Much Paint Do You Need for Your Room?
description: Calculate exactly how much paint (in liters) you need to paint a room. Automatically deduct doors and windows. Includes paint type, coats, and bucket sizes.
intro: Calculating paint for a whole room is more than just m² × coverage rate: you need to **deduct windows and doors**, account for **coats of paint**, and consider **color blocking**. If you're painting a dark color over light, you'll need more coats (and more paint). This calculator takes your room measurements, subtracts openings, and calculates the **exact liters and buckets** of paint needed.
keyTakeaway: **A 3×4 m room**: roughly 4 walls = ~28 m². With 2 coats of interior latex (12 m²/L), you need **~5 liters**. If you're switching from light to dark color, add a 3rd coat.
useCases: You're painting a bedroom and need to know how many liters to buy.
You're budgeting paint for your entire home.
You want to know if a 4L bucket is enough or if you need a 10L one.
You're painting a dark color over white walls and need to know how many coats you'll need.
You need to calculate paint while accounting for doors and windows.

faq.q: How much paint do I need for a 3×4 meter room?
faq.a: A 3×4 m room with 2.6 m high walls has about 28 m² of wall space (roughly 24 m² after subtracting 1 door and 1 window). With 2 coats of interior latex: **4–5 liters**. A single 4L bucket is usually just enough.

faq.q: How much surface area does one liter of paint cover?
faq.a: Interior latex: **10–12 m²/L** per coat. Exterior latex: 8–10 m²/L. Synthetic enamel: 12–14 m²/L. These are averages; wall texture and porosity make a big difference.

faq.q: How many coats of paint do I need?
faq.a: **2 coats** is the standard for good coverage and durability. If you're painting a dark color over light (or vice versa), use **3 coats** for proper hiding. A single coat works only for touch-ups on the same color.

faq.q: Do I need to use primer before painting?
faq.a: Yes, primer is essential on **new drywall, raw wood, or very porous surfaces**. Primer seals porosity and improves adhesion. If walls are already painted and in good shape, primer isn't always necessary—but it helps with color changes.

faq.q: What's the standard size of a door and window?
faq.a: Standard interior door: **0.80 × 2.00 m = 1.6 m²**. Standard window: **1.50 × 1.20 m = 1.8 m²**. This calculator automatically deducts these from your wall area.

faq.q: Should I thin my paint to make it go further?
faq.a: Thinning paint stretches it further but reduces coverage and durability. For the first coat, thin by up to **10% with water** (for latex). The final coat should be undiluted for maximum coverage and finish quality.

faq.q: How long does it take to paint a room?
faq.a: One person can paint a 3×4 m room in **4–6 hours** with 2 coats, including drying time between coats. Professional painters finish in 2–3 hours. Drying between coats typically takes 2–4 hours with latex paint.

faq.q: What's the difference between interior and exterior paint?
faq.a: Exterior paint has more binders and UV protection for weather resistance but covers less area per liter (~9 m²/L vs. ~11 m²/L). Interior latex is more forgiving, cheaper, and has better color options. Never use interior paint outside.

faq.q: Can I paint a light color over a dark color?
faq.a: Yes, but it requires 3 coats minimum with light-colored paint because dark colors bleed through. Using a primer helps: one primer coat + two paint coats is often more economical than three coats of paint alone.

explanation:
## How Much Paint Do You Need?

Calculating paint for a room involves more than multiplying m² by coverage rate. You must **deduct openings** (doors ~1.6 m², windows ~1.8 m²), account for **number of coats**, and adjust for **paint type** and wall porosity.

## Coverage by Paint Type

| Type | m²/liter (per coat) |
|---|---|
| Interior latex | 11–13 |
| Exterior latex | 9–11 |
| Synthetic enamel | 14–16 |
| Acrylic enamel | 12–14 |
| Primer/sealer | 15–18 |

## Complete Formula

**m² of walls = (2 × length + 2 × width) × height – openings**

Then: **liters needed = (m² of walls × coats) / coverage rate**

Standard coats: **2** for color matching, **3** when switching from dark to light.

## Tips and Common Mistakes

- **Don't skip primer** on new drywall or highly absorbent surfaces.
- **Add 10–15% buffer** for touch-ups and waste.
- **Buy in bulk**: a 20L bucket is more cost-effective per liter than multiple 4L buckets for whole-house projects.
- **Ceiling paint separately**: ceilings often need matte finish primer or specialized paint.

example.title: Real example: Living room 4 × 3 × 2.6 m with 1 door and 1 window
steps: **Inputs**: length=4, width=3, height=2.6, 1 door, 1 window, 2 coats, interior latex.
**Recommended purchase**: 1 × 4L bucket + 1 × 2L bucket, or 1 × 10L bucket (extra for touch-ups).
example.result: About 5.5 liters of interior latex covers 2 coats on a standard living room. Buy 6L for margin and touch-ups.
